signals.headline: Inflation trajectory confirmed | YES | HIGH confidence**
signals.raw:
Inflation trajectory confirmed | YES | HIGH confidence**
Core PCE inflation remains stuck at 2.9% (2025) with IMF projections of 2.6% by end-2026—well above the 2% target. The IMF flagged "upside risks to global commodity prices" from the Iran conflict keeping oil elevated.[1]

signals.headline: GDP growth projection | YES | HIGH confidence**
signals.raw:
GDP growth projection | YES | HIGH confidence**
The IMF projects modest U.S. GDP growth of 2.4% in 2026, with tariffs expected to reduce activity and create "sizable negative spillovers" on trading partners.[1]

These April 2 developments would likely move markets given the hawkish Fed stance, elevated tariff rates at 43-year highs, and sticky inflation—but I cannot confirm what has occurred since

signals.headline: IMF Article IV Consultation findings (April 2, 2026) | YES | HIGH confidence**
signals.raw:
IMF Article IV Consultation findings (April 2, 2026) | YES | HIGH confidence**
The IMF's Executive Board explicitly stated there is "little room to cut interest rates in 2026," with rate cuts only appropriate if labor market conditions materially worsen alongside declining inflation. The Fed funds rate is projected to move only from 3.6% to 3.4% in 2026.[1]
matched_markets: Will Powell say "Tariff inflation" during April press conference?

signals.headline: Tariff rate confirmation (April 2, 2026) | YES | HIGH confidence**
signals.raw:
Tariff rate confirmation (April 2, 2026) | YES | HIGH confidence**
The U.S. average effective tariff rate stands at **11.0%, the highest since 1943** (excluding 2025). If Section 122 tariffs expire as scheduled in 150 days, the rate will fall to 8.2%.[2]
